title: INDEX + MATCH
summary: INDEX returns a value at a given position in a range, and MATCH finds the position of a value. Combined, they create a flexible lookup that can search in any direction.
jobUseCases: Looking up a value to the left of the search column, which VLOOKUP cannot do
Building two-way lookups that match both a row and a column header
Returning employee details that stay correct even when columns are inserted
Fetching the latest price where the key column sits to the right of the result
Creating robust report templates that survive structural changes to the data

examples.title: Basic INDEX + MATCH
examples.explanation: MATCH finds the row number where 'E102' appears in A2:A100, then INDEX returns the value at that row in C2:C100. The 0 in MATCH forces an exact match.

examples.title: Left lookup
examples.explanation: Finds 'Priya' in column C and returns the value from column A to its left, something VLOOKUP cannot achieve.

examples.title: Two-way lookup (row and column)
examples.explanation: The first MATCH locates the employee row and the second MATCH locates the 'March' column, so INDEX returns the value at their intersection.

examples.title: Lookup from input cells
examples.explanation: Looks up whatever ID is in H2 and returns the matching value from column D. Absolute references keep the ranges fixed when copied down.

commonMistakes: Omitting the 0 in MATCH, which defaults to approximate match and needs sorted data
Mismatching the height of the INDEX return_range and the MATCH lookup_range so rows misalign
Pointing MATCH at the return column instead of the column that holds the lookup value
Forgetting absolute references, causing ranges to shift when the formula is copied
Overcomplicating simple right-side lookups where XLOOKUP would be clearer
practiceTips: Build MATCH first and confirm it returns the correct position number before nesting it in INDEX
Use INDEX + MATCH when you need left lookups or robustness against inserted columns
For two-way lookups, keep one MATCH for the row and one for the column header
